data.title: Materialise NV 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Materialise NV in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: T-Mobile Q2 Earnings: Convergence Changes The Game
data.description:
I am updating my previous analysis on T-Mobile (NASDAQ:TMUS) in light of Q2 2025 earnings which were released post-market on Wednesday, July 23rd. I previously rated T-Mobile a sell for the following reasons:

  • DCF-analysis supported a price target of $226, 14% downside to pricing at the time.
  • The industry was on the verge of price wars as consumer confidence fell and household debt increased
  • Industry forecasts were already being scaled back and T-Mobile's ARPU was leveling off

Since then, T-Mobile has returned 9% while the S&P 500 has returned over 12%.

I have been bearish on T-Mobile the last few quarters, not because of the company's strength, but because of the elevated share price. After two quarters in a row of exceptional performance, it was already getting hard to maintain this stance. But specifically digging into convergence results on top of higher cash flow guidance, my DCF analysis also supports a more positive rating.

At a price target of $287, 15% upside from today's pricing and revised up from my prior price target of $226, I raise my rating from sell to buy.

Q2 Earnings Recap

T-Mobile had another exceptional quarter with EPS of $2.86 beating consensus by $0.17.

The business really outperformed from a volume standpoint with postpaid metrics and 5G broadband metrics up both sequentially and year-over-year. The 5G performance especially caught my attention and drives the focus of the rest of my analysis.

The biggest news was T-Mobile revising guidance upward, signaling that Q2 was not an outlier but a continuation of a trend.

Given the economic and market risks facing T-Mobile, I was truly impressed by the earnings across the board and it has caused me to revisit my assumptions and scrub everything from top to bottom.

Valuation

I updated my ongoing DCF analysis for T-Mobile with the following assumptions:

  • 5% near-term revenue growth based on guidance, trends, and convergence trajectory
  • 3% near-term cost growth based on inflation forecast and mid-point of management guidance
  • 2.7% long-run growth rate, one percentage point below the market CAGR to hedge for scale of cost base and declining returns over time
  • 7% discount rate based on 6.8% estimated WACC
  • Consistent with management guidance, I included Metronet and excluded US Cellular

This DCF analysis yields a price target of $287, 15% upside from today's pricing and up from my prior price target of $226.

Wall Street is also optimistic with a price target of $269, more than 7% upside to today's pricing. I noted that my price target is still within the overall Wall Street range.

The quant rating is at strong buy driven by T-Mobile's exception growth and profitability the last few quarters.

Convergence Has Changed The Game

My bear case for T-Mobile had been the risk of a price war outweighing the upside from the company's strong execution. With the US Telecom market forecast to grow just a little ahead of inflation, price war risk is high in what is essentially a 3-player postpaid market.

While that risk still exists, it is impossible to ignore T-Mobile's execution. And what really changes my thesis is T-Mobile's performance in convergence with their 5G broadband product. I use this AT&T (T) chart far too often but I love the clear story. Convergence not only drives growth, it drives happier and stickier customers which is critical in a slow growth environment.

T-Mobile's broadband business is not only accelerating from a net addition standpoint, they are experiencing lower churn in alignment with AT&Ts research above. This is especially significant as, unlike AT&T and Verizon (VZ), T-Mobile doesn't have an existing broadband offer so this represents incremental accounts on lower invested capital.

I had also been worried about promotions and price wars as ARPA and ARPU showed weakness in Q4 2024 and Q1 2025. While we are not out of the woods yet, T-Mobile was able to return to growth. This means they were able to overcome promotional activity with premium services, while also not giving up the farm to grow broadband.

With no legacy copper decay to worry about, and essentially pure upside from 5G broadband, the trends in T-Mobile have changed the game raising both my revenue growth expectations as well as cash flow expectations. All of this aligns with managements guidance for 2025, but I am comfortable extrapolating this to outer years based on my convergence research and the overall market expectations.

Downside Risk

Downside risk continues to stem from price wars which I am closely monitoring. Historical price wars have impacted telecom companies, besides the obvious "lower revenue." The last major pricing action for wireless was in the '16 to '17 timeframe.

While T-Mobile was smaller and pre-Sprint at the time, AT&T and Verizon both saw a material drop in normalized net income, that was largely not recovered until the '21 to '22 timeframe. So historically, major price wars have had bottom-line impacts with multi-year recovery.

So why the risk of a price war now? Consumer confidence in the US fell significantly in February 2025 as a result of uncertainty around mass government layoffs alongside tariff policy. Household debt has also increased, signaling consumers are being strapped financially.

Industry observers also expect the telecom industry to have a bumpy ride. In a recent study from Deloitte, Fixed Wireless Access (a major growth business for the major telecoms) is expected to slow.

While FWA is expected to expand globally, providing connectivity to millions, the US market will likely experience more measured growth.

I believe T-Mobile is best positioned to weather the storm, but I still intend to keep a close eye on ARPU and promotion activity.

Verdict

It is impossible to ignore T-Mobile's outsized performance relative to competitors. This comes in addition to exceptional performance in convergence as the 5G broadband business accelerates. While price wars continue to be a concern, increased guidance and improved trends swing the math bullish on T-Mobile.

At a price target of $287, 15% upside from today's pricing and revised up from my prior price target of $226, I raise my rating from sell to buy.

data.title: CNX Resources Corporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by CNX Resources Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Fidelity Growth Company Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares (MUTF:FDGRX) gained 18.97%, outpacing the 17.55% advance of the benchmark, the Russell 3000* Growth Index. As always, we focused on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches that we believe are capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Long-term performance continues to favor the fund over the benchmark. U.S. growth stocks ended June at an all-time high after erasing a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan, resulting in rising volatility and concern about a global trade war. The benchmark returned -12.75% from April 2 through April 8, but then sharply reversed course and rose 32.41% through midyear.

The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2% target. These tailwinds overshadowed rising tension in the Middle East and lingering uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause.

The Fed held steady its benchmark federal funds rate in May and June, as expected, stating it is open to cutting rates in the second half of the year but prefers a wait-and-see stance for the time being, given its expectation for meaningful inflation in the coming months, as well as uncertainty about the impact of tariffs on prices. The central bank cut its projection for U.S. economic growth this year and raised its outlook for inflation and unemployment.

For the quarter, growth stocks handily outpaced value, as the Russell 3000* Value Index gained 3.84%. Accordingly, the index's gain was concentrated in the growth-oriented information technology ( +25% ) and communication services ( +24% ) sectors, mostly reflecting the potential for generative artificial intelligence to drive transformative change. These two groups combined represented about 60% of the index for the three months. Stocks in the consumer discretionary and industrials sectors, which typically move in step with the economy, each rose 14 %.

In contrast, energy ( -7% ) - a very small index component - lagged by the widest margin, as the price of oil rose. The defensive health care sector also lost ground, returning roughly -1 %.

Stock selection meaningfully contributed to the fund's performance versus the benchmark for the quarter, led by our picks in the information technology and financials sectors.

In tech, a large overweight in Nvidia ( +46% ) was by far the top individual contributor. On May 28, the dominant maker of advanced graphics chips for generative AI systems reported sharp increases in sales and profit for the three months ending April 27. Revenue surged 69% to a new quarterly record, even though the company was effectively shut out of China, one of the largest markets for its products. Nvidia's data-center business grew even faster, rising 73% due to strong demand for chips and other components used in AI computing systems. Given our positive view of Nvidia's growth prospects, the stock ended June as the top holding and overweight by a wide margin.

Also in tech, it helped to hold a non-benchmark stake in CoreWeave, which specializes in providing cloud-based graphics processing unit infrastructure to AI developers. The position gained 335% the past three months, which roughly matched the period since its March 28 initial public offering. In mid-May, the company reported better-than-expected quarterly revenue, and management guided second-quarter sales higher than Wall Street's consensus expectation, reflecting strong growth driven by increased adoption of AI by enterprises.

In contrast, our decision to underweight chipmaker Broadcom (AVGO) ( +65% ) was the largest individual relative detractor. We preferred to overweight Nvidia and other semiconductor stocks. On June 5, the company announced that revenue and sales for the three months ending May 4 were slightly ahead of consensus expectations, driven by its custom chips and software that speed up AI computations. Also, management's revenue forecast for its next quarter exceeded analysts' forecasts. We'll note that we modestly added to Broadcom this quarter, making it a fairly sizable fund holding, but it was the second-largest underweight as of midyear.

An overweight in athletic apparel maker lululemon athletica (-16%) hurt relative performance. The stock fell sharply in June after the company warned its profit would be held back by macro challenges. We reduced the fund's stake in Q2, which nonetheless remained an overweight as of June 30.

* 1 basis point =0.01 %.

Outlook and Positioning

As the second half of 2025 begins, the global economy appears to be on steadier ground than many investors had anticipated and the near-term outlook for expansion looks favorable, but policy uncertainty is high, especially related to tariffs. Much of the actual and potential impact of tariff policy is ahead, in our view, and we recognize that in the past high policy uncertainty has tended to coincide with elevated market volatility.

The Fed, in a wait-and-see stance after reducing rates by 1 percentage point in 2024, is leaning toward more cuts, and a sweeping, pro-cyclical domestic policy bill is nearing passage.

Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yields finished June about where they began the quarter, around 4.2 %. Inflation remained higher than the Fed's 2% target, as sticky prices in services and housing offset deflation in goods.

Inflation has come down significantly from its 2022 peak, but we believe more-robust measures of the Fed's preferred inflation metric indicate persistent inflationary pressure under the surface. We expect inflation to remain rangebound around 3% into next year, with upside risk from tariff hikes.

The U.S. dollar dropped to a three-year low in Q2, extending its year-to-date decline to roughly 10%. Labor trends, including strong wage growth and a surge in consumption spending, moderated the past three months, as cuts to federal government funding, layoffs and rising uncertainty led to slower spending and a decline in consumer sentiment.

Business investment remained positive, spurred by outlays for Al by large companies in the information technology and communication services sectors. After weakening in Q1, the consensus estimate for 2025 corporate earnings growth stabilized, while profit expectations were robust.

Against this backdrop, we are focusing on investments with a favorable outlook, even amid modest economic growth, while noting additional risks are probable. We plan to be more selective than ever in choosing stocks.

In all sectors, the fund holds companies with above-benchmark, long-term growth in sales and earnings per share, reflecting our view that growth for advantaged companies can persist for years. As a result, changes to the fund's positioning were modest in Q2, although the June rebalance of the fund's benchmark impacted some relative sector weightings.

For instance, although we slightly added to the fund's allocation to information technology, the sector ended June as a modest underweight. Still, the sector remained by far the largest area of investment, at 49% of assets.

Within tech, the fund holds above-benchmark holdings in niche companies that appear to be attractive, secular growers. This is partially offset by underweights in some larger, established companies that appear fairly valued and without compelling longterm growth prospects.

The second-largest area of investment was consumer discretionary, at 15% of assets, including key investments in athletic footwear and apparel makers that are executing well, as well as online goods and service providers taking share from physical merchants.

As of midyear, the fund's largest overweights were in health care, consumer discretionary and communication services, whereas the biggest underweights were industrials and financials.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity" Growth Company Fund is a domestic equity strategy that invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue chip to aggressive growth.
  • Our investment approach is anchored by the philosophy that the market often underestimates the duration of a company's growth, particularly in cases where the resiliency and extensibility of the business model are underappreciated.
  • We focus on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches that we believe are capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth.
  • This approach typically leads us to companies that we think have the potential to unlock shareholder value through either a growth-enhancing product cycle or an internal catalyst, such as a turnaround or an acquisition.
  • We believe it critical that companies fund their own growth - through the cash they generate and benefit from management teams focused on creating long-term shareholder value.

Definitions and Important Information

Unless otherwise expressly disclosed to you in writing, the information provided in this material is for educational purposes only. Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you. Fidelity and its representatives may have a conflict of interest in the products or services mentioned in this material because they have a financial interest in the products or services and may receive compensation, directly or indirectly, in connection with the management, distribution, and/or servicing of these products or services, including Fidelity funds, certain third-party funds and products, and certain investment services. Before making any investment decisions, you should take into account all of the particular facts and circumstances of your or your client's individual situation and reach out to a professional adviser, if applicable.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share Growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book (P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity. Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Russell 3000 Growth Index is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the performance of the broad growth segment of the US equity market. It includes those Russell 3000 Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth rates. Effective March 24, 2025, the index applies a capping methodology. Index constituents are capped quarterly so that no more than 22.5% of the indexes weight may be allocated to a single constituent, and the sum of the weights of all constituents representing more than 4.5% of the index should not exceed 45% of the total index weight. For periods prior to March 24, 2025, the index was uncapped.

Russell 3000 Value Index is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the performance of the broad value segment of the U.S. equity market. It includes those Russell 3000 Index companies with a lower price-to-book ratio and lower forecasted growth rate.

MARKET-SEGMENT WEIGHTS

Market-segment weights illustrate examples of sectors or industries in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any sector or industry.

RANKING INFORMATION

(C) 2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. The Morningstar information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or redistributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses. % Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category. The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The topperforming fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1% .% Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

RELATIVE WEIGHTS

Relative weights represents the% of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. RSquared ranges from 0 , meaning no correlation, to 1 , meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Views expressed are through the end of the period stated and do not necessarily represent the views of Fidelity. data.title: BNP Paribas SA 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by BNP Paribas SA in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: 3 Ways To Win With Summit Hotel Properties' Preferred F Shares
data.description:
Summit Hotel Properties (NYSE:INN), like most hotel REITs, is operating in a challenging environment of high cost and high competition. Despite trading at a massive discount to net asset value and at only 12X AFFO, I would be hesitant to buy common shares. The F series preferred, however, strikes me as highly opportunistic with 8.4% current yield, 42% upside to par and ample dividend coverage.

We see 3 ways for preferred series F investors to win:

  1. Continuing as is, just collecting dividends
  2. Buyout of INN at any price over $3.50
  3. Interest rates decline triggering price rise toward $25

Let us begin by examining the reliability of the preferred regarding earnings support of dividends.

INN cashflow amount and stability

In REIT accounting, AFFO per share refers to the amount of AFFO left for common AFTER paying the preferred dividends. As such, the preferred dividends are covered as long as AFFO/share is greater than $0.

That said, we like to verify 2 factors to feel secure about a preferred’s dividend:

  1. A significant buffer such that preferred is still covered if cashflows falter
  2. Clean accounting on AFFO figure

INN’s latest earnings report shows $0.22 of AFFO for 1Q25. Annualized that would be $0.88, but hotels are a seasonal business. Per INN’s history and hotels broadly, the 2nd and 3rd quarters are typically the strongest.

Adjusting for seasonality, the annualized pace appears to be over $0.90 a share which would be ample coverage for the preferreds.

However, there are some anomalies in the company’s calculation of AFFO such that it doesn’t represent true earnings.

Any time a REIT shows AFFO higher than FFO, something is probably funky with their calculation. In this case, the company is adding back deferred financing costs, amortization of franchise fees, and stock compensation.

While these are not cash expenses in the present quarter, they are real expenses and detract from real earnings.

We would take those out of our AFFO calculation.

Additionally, hotels are a capital-intensive business. Renovations are required on a regular basis to comply with brand standards. INN’s 10-Q cites 2025 capex expectations:

“We anticipate spending approximately $60.0 million to $70.0 million on capital expenditures on a pro rata basis during 2025.”

That is roughly $0.60 per share.

The way in which to account for this is a bit of a judgement call. It is a cash expense, but it is also not a total waste. It does preserve, and in some cases, increase the value of their properties. Thus, I would tend to count some portion of it as an expense which would deduct from earnings and some portion of it as capital investment.

Sell side analysts have their own accounting for INN’s AFFO which shows it drastically lower than what the company reports.

I think the $0.42 cent figure is about right.

That figure would represent taking away the wonky add-backs we discussed earlier as well as a moderate portion of the $65 million expected capex.

At the $0.42 annual AFFO level, INN’s preferred are still well covered. Even a downturn in a normal business cycle would not reduce AFFO by that much. We feel quite comfortable with dividend security of INN’s preferreds.

Preferred safety from liquidation preference perspective

Preferreds are safest when there is a massive amount of equity below them and minimal debt above them within the capital stack. INN has just over $600 million in common equity and close to $700 million including 13 million OP units.

Beyond the absolute numbers, ratios significantly impact how quickly equity can deteriorate in adverse scenarios. As of 1Q25, INN has debt to gross properties of about 39%. That is a fairly typical level for real estate. The company has been reducing leverage in the past few years.

Strategic dispositions of assets have funded much of the debt reduction.

With moderate debt and a decent equity cushion, the liquidation preference of INN preferreds is reasonably secure.

Return potential of INN preferreds

INN has both a series E and series F preferred. We tabulate the vital statistics on the Portfolio Income Solutions preferred tracker.

On the surface, the preferreds look quite similar with the E having slightly more current yield (8.58% versus 8.37%) in exchange for the F having slightly more upside to par (42.5% versus 37.3%). Based on the pricing and coupons I would normally consider these to be about equally opportunistic.

However, they have significantly different change of control provisions that, in my opinion, make the F series better.

With both preferreds, a change of control triggers an option for INN to redeem them for cash equal to liquidation preference ($25 per share plus accrued dividends). Given these preferreds trading in the high teens, that would be a great outcome. Any shares not redeemed by INN in such an event will then be at the option of the preferred holder to convert into common shares up to a maximum of $25 plus accrued dividends.

Each series of preferreds has a fixed cap on the number of shares it can convert into.

  • Series E converts into a maximum of 3.1686 shares
  • Series F converts into a maximum of 5.8275 shares

At the current $5.59 price of INN common that implies a conversion value of $17.71 for series E and $32.57 for series F.

The $25 plus accrued dividend maximum means one wouldn’t realize the full $32.57, but at current pricing a change of control would provide series F holders with the liquidation value of $25.

In contrast, each series E preferred would only convert into $17.71 worth of common.

The delta gets even bigger if INN common share price declines. At $4.30 per share, the series F preferred still converts to a full $25 while the series E would just get $13.62.

As such, the change of control provisions provide the F series with both more upside and reduced risk as compared to series E.

3 ways to win with INN- F

The default scenario for INN-F is that business just continues as usual. Buying at today’s price would give a steady yield of 8.4%, which is strong compared to other fixed income securities.

Beyond the passive yield, we see 2 scenarios thatcwould amplify returns with significant capital appreciation.

  • Buyout of INN
  • Interest rates drop

Private equity has been sniffing around hotels a bit. The hotel industry has been slightly distressed since COVID with many hotels trading far below replacement cost and below NAV.

INN is among the juicier candidates for a buyout because it is trading at 58% of NAV.

Even with a normal buyout premium, the buyer could get INN’s hotel portfolio at a substantial discount.

Such a buyout would trigger the change of control provisions discussed above and as long as the buyout price is over $4.30, INN-F would get the full $25 liquidation preference. Note that with INN currently trading at $5.60, a buyout price above $4.30 is not much of a stretch.

If a buyout happens in the near term, it would clearly be higher than $5.60 to get INN shareholders to agree, but the discounted buyout scenarios are relevant too in case the company suffers distress which then induces them to be more willing to sell. The large buffer from series F’s conversion ratio makes buyback likely a favorable event even in some ugly scenarios.

Finally, INN-F could also deliver significant capital gains if interest rates go down. While we view INN preferreds as only moderate risk, that is enough risk to warrant a premium yield over “risk free” treasuries.

The market has deemed a roughly 400 basis point yield premium appropriate with the 10 year treasury fluctuating around 4.4%.

To the extent treasury yields drop, INN-F’s market price would rise to keep the premium roughly in parallel.

Ways to lose (risk factors)

While both cashflows and asset value have a nice cushion presently, certain fundamental factors could erode the buffer over time:

  • Sustained economic recession
  • Adverse changes in travel preferences
  • Increased supply of competing hotels or hotel-like properties

At current ratios fundamental harm would need to be quite significant to hurt the preferreds. Changes to capital structure could reduce the buffer. Notably, INN has registered for share buybacks. To the extent common share buybacks are executed, preferred coverage ratios get worse.

Overall take

INN-F offers a competitive current yield with significant capital gains potential. It could be interesting for those seeking steady dividend income.

data.title: Otis Worldwide: Headwinds Result In New 52-Week Low. Is Price Decline A Buying Opportunity?
data.description:
Introduction

Every time I step inside of an elevator, I look for the name Otis Worldwide (NYSE:OTIS) since I'm a fan and shareholder. While visiting back home recently, I was disappointed to see the hotel elevator was made by Schindler (OTC:SHLRF), an elevator company headquartered in New Jersey that trades on the Swiss stock exchange.

OTIS recently reported their Q2 earnings that resulted in a steep sell-off, with the stock hitting a new 52-week low of $86 a share.

And despite headwinds that are likely to linger for the near to medium term, OTIS' mitigation strategies, solid fundamentals, and sell-off could be viewed as a potential buying opportunity for long-term investors.

In this article, I discuss OTIS' recent earnings, headwinds, and why the stock could see further volatility in the coming months.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Otis Worldwide at the beginning of the year in January in an article titled: A Boring Dividend Stock I Just Bought.

Since then, the stock has seen some price appreciation and traded around $101 a share before suffering a steep sell-off after earnings, which I'll touch on later. As a result, OTIS is down 7.32% at the time of writing, compared to S&P (SP500), which is up nearly 5%.

After their then Q3'24 earnings, I upgraded them from a hold to a buy due to their resilience despite headwinds, particularly in their Asia region. OTIS still managed to see net sales growth of 1.2% and EPS growth of 9%.

Additionally, their solid balance sheet, dividend growth record, and upside to their end of 2026 price target of $115 made for a compelling investment case.

Why The Stock Sold Off

As previously mentioned, Otis Worldwide sold off after the release of their Q2 earnings. The elevator company delivered a decent report in my opinion, managing to beat analysts' estimates on their bottom line with a $0.02 beat. This amounted to $1.05. Revenue of $3.6 billion missed slightly by $10 million.

Although a decent earnings report on the surface, management revised their 2025 outlook. Sales are now expected to be in a range of $14.5 billion to $14.6 billion, down from $14.6 billion to $14.8 billion prior.

This is an obvious sign that their headwinds are lingering and will likely continue for the near to medium term, particularly from challenges in China.

Free cash flow expectations also dipped, with this now expected to be between $1.4 billion and $1.5 billion, down from $1.6 billion in the previous quarter. As a result, this caused the double-digit decline and the stock hitting a new 52-week low.

And as seen from the chart below, this was all negatively impacted by their New Equipment segment, which has plagued the company for some time now.

During my last thesis in January, new equipment orders dropped 8.2% year-over-year, driven by Asia, which dropped nearly 20%. But their Service segment saw decent portfolio growth of 4% in the quarter as a result of growth in all regions.

In their most recent quarter, equipment orders were again impacted by China, down 1%. Excluding this country, orders were actually up 11%. But sales again declined roughly 20%.

EMEA also saw a decline due to weaker demand in Europe. It also didn't help that OTIS' revenue growth was flat from the year prior and EPS saw a slight decline from $1.06.

But OTIS' Uplift Program, aimed at transforming company operations, is expected to mitigate this by saving the company an estimated $200 million this year.

They are also anticipating reducing costs with their China transformation program. Savings here are expected to be $40 million. And this should drive profitability going forward.

The President's tariffs have also impacted the company. But the good news is management stated during earnings that these are expected to impact them by $25 million to $35 million, down from $45 million to $75 million. These could also impact the company in the coming weeks as the President's August 1st tariff deadline is due to expire.

Solid EPS Growth & Buybacks

Despite their headwinds, OTIS still expects to see solid bottom line growth of 4%–7% growth from 2024's $3.83. This is now expected to be in the range of $4.00–$4.14.

Moreover, their continuous share repurchases will likely continue to drive growth. During the quarter, management repurchased roughly $300 million worth of shares, bringing year-to-date buybacks to approximately $550 million. For the year, management plans to repurchase $800 million (worth of shares).

Dividend Safety

Despite lowering free cash flow expectations and free cash flow declining from the previous year, OTIS' dividend remained well-covered. Free cash flow for the prior year's quarter was $353 million, a decline of $110 million in their most recent quarter.

For the first half of 2025, FCF amounted to $429 million, down from $508 million. This was also a result of higher CAPEX, which increased $15 million from $55 million to $70 million.

OTIS also raised their dividend in April by 8% to $0.42, lower than the previous year's 14.7%, another sign of challenging times. But using their shares outstanding of 396 million, their dividend was well-covered with a quarterly payout ratio of roughly 68.4%.

Using their dividend estimates of $1.58, this would give them a free cash flow payout ratio of roughly 39% if their share count remained flat. But due to frequent share repurchases, I expect this to be slightly lower.

Price Drop An Opportunity?

Long term, I think OTIS remains a compelling investment, especially if the stock experiences further volatility. Using their earnings midpoint, they still could be viewed as expensive with a forward P/E of 21.74x.

For comparison, OTIS' current forward P/E sits below peers Ingersoll Rand's (IR) and KONE Oyj's (OTCPK:KNYJY) 25.99x and 27.45x, respectively.

But this is currently below their 5-year average of 25.17x. And due to their competitive advantage of being the largest manufacturer of elevators and escalators, a P/E of 25x may be suitable for a company of its caliber despite current headwinds.

Additionally, the sell-off may be a buying opportunity. While I do suspect the stock could remain volatile until there's more economic clarity, long-term investors should see this as a potential buying opportunity as upside is significant over the next two years.

Bottom Line

Although Otis Worldwide's headwinds have lingered over the past year, their revised guidance led to their steep sell-off and the stock making a new 52-week low.

Moreover, I anticipate they could remain volatile in the near to medium term due to tariffs and higher for longer interest rates. These will likely continue to impact their New Equipment segment as a result.

But their fundamentals remain strong with a well-covered dividend and mitigation measures in place to offset declining segment revenues and softness in China.

Long term, I expect OTIS to get back to high single-digit to double-digit growth. And their sell-off should be seen as a buying opportunity if you're a buy-and-hold investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Western Asset GSM 7-Year Portfolios Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The fixed income market experienced periods of volatility, but posted a modest gain during the second quarter. Investor sentiment was impacted by several factors, including persistent trade uncertainty, signs of global economic strains, questions over future Federal Reserve’s (Fed) monetary policy, mounting fiscal concerns, and hostilities between Israel and Iran.
  • Contributors: Duration positioning was beneficial for performance.
  • Detractors: There were no meaningful detractors from returns over the period.
  • Outlook: Global growth is expected to slow given heightened unpredictability, but should remain positive. Overall monetary policy remains restrictive and we believe that central banks will continue to cut rates. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. While fundamentals remain positive, spreads are at the tight end of historical ranges in some sectors and warrant caution.

Portfolio Characteristics

Portfolio

Effective Duration

3.60 Years

Sector Allocation (% of Fixed Income)

Portfolio

Treasury

100.00

Performance Review

  • Tactical duration positioning, which was net long overall, contributed to performance.
  • There were no meaningful detractors from returns over the period.
  • Yield curve positioning, sector allocation and security selection did not meaningfully impact the strategy’s relative performance during the quarter.

Outlook

  • U.S. government policy has caused severe volatility in fixed-income markets over the last several months. Global growth is expected to slow given heightened unpredictability, but should remain positive. U.S. growth is downshifting due to a myriad of factors, including tariff uncertainty, waning benefits from immigration, and reduced government spending in recent years. A significant fiscal boost from European defense and German infrastructure spending should support eurozone growth and provide relief from tariff-related uncertainty. Deflationary pressures in China persist and confidence is weak amid property market concerns, but sentiment is improving with fiscal stimulus and policy easing.
  • Overall monetary policy remains restrictive and we believe that central banks will continue to cut rates. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen further given concerns over fiscal policies.
  • While fundamentals remain positive, spreads are at the tight end of historical ranges in some sectors and warrant caution. We will continue to look for further periods of volatility to add to spread products.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. An investment in this strategy can lose value. Please visit www.franklintempleton.com for the latest performance figures. Investors cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges.

Fees: Periods less than one year are not annualized. Performance results are for the composite which includes all actual, fully discretionary accounts with substantially similar investment policies and objectives managed to the composite’s investment strategy. Composite returns are stated in U.S. dollars and assume reinvestment of any dividends, interest income, capital gains, or other earnings. The composite may include account(s) that are gross of fees and pure gross of fees. “Pure” gross-of-fee returns do not reflect the deduction of any expenses, including transaction costs. A traditional (or “true”) gross-of-fee return reflects performance after the reduction of transaction costs but before the reduction of the investment advisory fee. The gross-of- fee return may include a blend of “true” gross-of-fee returns for non-wrap accounts and “pure” gross-of-fee returns for wrap accounts. Net-of-fee returns is reduced by a model “wrap fee” (3.0% is the maximum anticipated wrap fee for equity and balanced portfolios) which includes trading expenses as well as investment management, administrative and custodial fees. The model wrap fee used represents the highest anticipated wrap fee applicable to the strategy. Actual fees and account minimums may vary.

For fee schedules, contact your financial professional, or if you enter into an agreement directly with Franklin Templeton Private Portfolio Group, LLC (“FTPPG”), refer to FTPPG’s Form ADV Part 2A disclosure document. Management and performance of individual accounts may vary for reasons that include the existence of different implementation practices and model requirements in different investment programs.

Western Asset Management Company, LLC claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®). GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein.

To obtain specific information on available products and services or a GIPS Report, contact your Franklin Templeton separately managed account sales team at (800) DIAL BEN/342-5236.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks.

Glossary

The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

The eurozone, officially known as the euro area, is a geographic and economic region that consists of all the European Union countries that have fully incorporated the euro as their national currency.

The Federal Reserve Board (“Fed”) is responsible for the formulation of policies designed to promote economic growth, full employment, stable prices and a sustainable pattern of international trade and payments.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or portfolio. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the portfolio’s selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

These materials are being provided for illustrative and informational purposes only. The information contained herein is obtained from multiple sources that are believed to be reliable. However, such information has not been verified, and may be different from the information included in documents and materials created by the sponsor firm in whose investment program a client participates. Some sponsor firms may require that these materials be preceded or accompanied by investment profiles or other documents or materials prepared by such sponsor firms, which will be provided upon a client’s request. For additional information, documents and/or materials, please speak to your Financial Professional or contact your sponsor firm.

Franklin Templeton (FT) is not undertaking to provide impartial advice. Nothing herein is intended to provide fiduciary advice. FT has a financial interest.

The Bloomberg U.S. Intermediate Treasury Index is composed of all public obligations of the U.S. Treasury. This index has an average current maturity of 3.69 years (average maturity may vary over time). Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Efiective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes.

Separately Managed Accounts (SMAs) are investment services provided by Franklin Templeton Private Portfolio Group, LLC (FTPPG), a federally registered investment advisor. Client portfolios are managed based on investment instructions or advice provided by one or more of the following Franklin Templeton affiliated sub-advisors: Western Asset Management Company, LLC.
